chunk_summary: This section outlines tele-nutrition protocols for triaging suspected severe acute malnutrition (SAM) in children aged 6–59 months, emphasizing early identification, urgent referral, and caregiver education to ensure safe stabilization and follow-up care.
learning_objectives: Understand the criteria for identifying suspected severe acute malnutrition (SAM) in children aged 6–59 months.
Learn tele-triage protocols for assessing danger signs and determining referral urgency.
Recognize the importance of caregiver education and interim safety measures during referral.
Familiarize with community-based protocols and their limitations in India.
key_facts: Suspected SAM in children aged 6–59 months includes MUAC <115 mm, very low weight-for-height/length, or bilateral pitting oedema.
Presence of any danger sign or oedema necessitates urgent referral to a stabilization facility.
Danger signs include lethargy, fever, hypothermia, respiratory distress, persistent vomiting, dehydration, and severe anemia.
Interim advice includes small frequent feeds, maintaining warmth, and avoiding force-feeding during travel to a facility.
Community-based protocols may be possible for uncomplicated SAM, but availability of therapeutic foods and trained programs varies by state in India.
reference_quote: Severe acute malnutrition (SAM) can deteriorate rapidly due to hypoglycemia, hypothermia, infection, and electrolyte imbalance, so tele-nutrition should prioritize early identification and urgent referral rather than remote 'treatment.'
document_key_topics: Severe acute malnutrition (SAM) identification and triage
Tele-nutrition protocols for referral and stabilization
Caregiver education and interim safety measures
